headline: Argentina first agent baseline. May 1 2026 Plaza de Mayo Labor Day protest was largest union march of Milei presidency against Feb 2026 Senate-approved labor reforms (12-hour workdays, extended probation, overtime-replacement). 200,000+ formal jobs lost since Milei took office; unemployment 7.5%. Documented violent suppression of protesters including pensioners. Supreme Court constitutional breach via unconstitutional judicial appointments. Amnesty flagged human rights crisis. Published 48.4 / Functional band sits boundary-adjacent (could land in Developing if INT and BND drop further). Compound evidence supports a modest downgrade to 43.8 — INT -0.5 (declared rights commitments contradicted by judicial appointments and pensioner-protester violence) and BND -0.5 (labor-rights consent erosion). Functional band sustained. Recommend downgrade with boundary-case approval.
boundary_note: Sub-threshold delta (-4.6 points) under the 5-point material-change rule. Confirm at 48.4 with documented dimension-pressure notation. Boundary-case approved: low confidence, evidence consistent with downward pressure but does not yet meet the 5-point threshold for material change. Re-evaluate within 30 days if labor reforms operationalize with documented harm scaling.
key_evidence: May 1 2026 Plaza de Mayo Labor Day protest: largest union march of Milei presidency.
February 2026 Senate-approved labor reforms: 12-hour workdays permitted, extended probation period, overtime replaceable with time-off.
200,000+ formal jobs lost since Milei took office (Dec 2023); unemployment 7.5%.
Documented violent suppression of pensioner-led protests (HRIUI March 2026 report).
Supreme Court constitutional breach via unconstitutional judicial appointments (HRIUI documentation).
Amnesty International flagged human rights crisis amid crackdown on protests and erosion of judicial independence.

evidence.finding: Plaza de Mayo May Day protests largest union march of Milei era; opposition to labor law overhaul.
Senate approved Milei-backed labor reforms; 12-hour workdays, extended probation, overtime replacement.

evidence.source: HRIUI March 2026 — Argentina human rights crisis
evidence.finding: Pensioner-protester violence; unconstitutional judicial appointments; protest crackdown pattern.

scoring_rationale.AWR: 3.0 confirmed under pressure. A1 Detection: civil society and HRIUI active; A4 Amplification: protest visibility sustained. Documented downward pressure from protest-suppression but published anchor holds.
scoring_rationale.EQU: 2.5 confirmed. Distributional erosion via labor reform impact.
scoring_rationale.BND: 3.0 confirmed under pressure. Labor-rights consent erosion via Feb 2026 reforms. Documented downward pressure.
scoring_rationale.ACC: 3.0 confirmed under pressure. Judicial-independence erosion documented but Court still functioning.
scoring_rationale.INT: 3.0 confirmed under pressure. Declared democratic-rights commitments contradicted by unconstitutional judicial appointments. Documented downward pressure.

math_check.validator_check: Reconstruction matches published 48.4 exactly. No math-hygiene flag.

negativeSignals: Labor law overhaul (12-hour days, extended probation); 200,000+ formal job losses; unemployment 7.5%; pensioner-protester violence; unconstitutional judicial appointments; Amnesty-flagged HR crisis.
positiveSignals: Functioning democratic institutions; active independent press; large-scale union mobilization permitted (May 1); macroeconomic stabilization indicators (inflation reduction, currency stability) — contested human cost.
sector_context: Latin America compound cluster with Colombia (UN OHCHR urgent action) and Ecuador (Freedom House security-state). Argentina distinguished by labor-rights pivot under Milei rather than security-driven repression.
open_actions_resolved: Labor reform impact, judicial appointments, protest-suppression absorbed into dimension-pressure notation
open_actions_carried: 30-day re-evaluation: if labor reforms operationalize with documented harm scaling
Watch: Supreme Court rulings on constitutional appointment challenges
